contact.office: The White House, 1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC 20500

alignment.label: Antagonist
alignment.summary: Significant misalignment on housing regulation, climate, and governance philosophy. Federal housing program cuts directly impact California's affordable housing pipeline.
key_agreements: Rhetoric around cutting red tape for construction and permitting
key_disagreements: Gutting federal housing programs (CDBG, LIHTC) that fund OC projects
Undermining transit investment and FTA grants
Anti-California posture threatens state housing mandate enforcement
Erosion of government transparency and institutional norms
alignment.engagement_strategy: Minimal direct engagement value. Monitor federal funding cuts that impact OC housing and transit. Advocate through California YIMBY and national housing orgs. Focus energy on state and local officials where impact is highest.

officials.name: JD Vance
officials.title: Vice President of the United States

contact.office: The White House, 1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC 20500

alignment.label: Antagonist
alignment.summary: Has expressed some pro-housing supply views historically but overall direction conflicts with pragmatic urbanism.
key_agreements: Has acknowledged housing supply constraints publicly
key_disagreements: Populist economic framing conflicts with market-oriented housing reform
Anti-institutional stance undermines good-government goals
alignment.engagement_strategy: No direct engagement value. Track any housing-related Senate tie-breaking votes.

officials.name: Gavin Newsom
officials.title: Governor of California

alignment.label: Ally
alignment.summary: Key YIMBY ally at the state level. His enforcement of RHNA compliance and Builder's Remedy is the single most important lever for housing production in OC. March 25 final warning to 15 noncompliant cities shows enforcement posture. Termed out 2027 — governor's race will shape future enforcement.
key_agreements: Aggressive RHNA enforcement against noncompliant cities
Builder's Remedy as housing production tool
Government transparency and open data initiatives
Signed SB 9, SB 10 and other housing supply bills
key_disagreements: Some housing policies lean more toward mandated affordability than pure supply
Budget deficits raise fiscal discipline concerns
Ambitious rhetoric sometimes exceeds implementation
alignment.engagement_strategy: Support enforcement actions vocally — submit comments when HCD reviews OC cities' housing elements. The governor's RHNA enforcement is your strongest ally in forcing local compliance. Track the 2026 governor's race candidates on housing enforcement continuity. California's state framework is an ally, not an enemy — in OC, you're pushing local government to comply with state mandates.

officials.notes: Termed out Jan 2027. Governor's race Nov 2026 — who replaces Newsom shapes RHNA enforcement.

officials.name: Board of Supervisors Chair
officials.title: OC Board of Supervisors Chair (rotating)

contact.office: Hall of Administration, 333 W Santa Ana Blvd, Santa Ana, CA 92701

alignment.label: Mixed Ally
alignment.summary: Current 3-2 Democratic majority is favorable. Chair rotates annually. The board's composition after June 2026 elections is the most critical variable for OC governance.
key_agreements: Current majority supports affordable housing initiatives
key_disagreements: Board is a coalition — not all members align on housing supply vs. affordability mandates
alignment.engagement_strategy: The Board of Supervisors controls the county budget, land use in unincorporated areas, and public health. Attend Tuesday meetings. The 3-2 majority is at stake in June 2026 — Districts 4 and 5 are the battleground. Your engagement with those races is the highest-leverage action in OC.

officials.notes: 3-2 Democratic majority. Districts 4 and 5 on ballot June 2026.

contact.office: City Hall, 300 E Chapman Ave, Orange, CA 92866

alignment.label: Unknown
alignment.summary: Nonpartisan mayor. City of Orange's Housing Element compliance status is the key indicator — the city appears to have achieved compliance (not on Newsom's March 25 noncompliant list), but 696 Builder's Remedy units from noncompliance period are still in play.
key_agreements: City appears to be moving toward Housing Element compliance
key_disagreements: Historical resistance to density in some neighborhoods
Builder's Remedy applications have been contentious
alignment.engagement_strategy: Attend city council meetings (2nd and 4th Tuesdays, 7pm). Research the mayor's voting record on housing and development items. The Housing Element compliance question is the top local issue — push for full compliance and support for Builder's Remedy projects. Nov 2026 mayor's race is an opportunity.

contact.office: 333 Bush St, Suite 3225, San Francisco, CA 94104

alignment.label: Ally
alignment.summary: Pro-housing, pro-transit, pro-infrastructure. Generally aligned on policy outcomes though more progressive on economic regulation than Doug prefers.
key_disagreements: More progressive on economic regulation
Rent control support conflicts with supply-side approach
alignment.engagement_strategy: Contact on federal housing and transit votes. Padilla's office in California is responsive to constituent input. Push for YIMBY Act and federal zoning reform incentives. Frame housing supply as complementary to affordability.

alignment.label: Mixed Ally
alignment.summary: Strong on government transparency and accountability — his signature issue. Generally pro-housing and pro-transit at the federal level.
key_agreements: Champion of government transparency and accountability
key_disagreements: May prioritize national security/governance reform over housing policy
Progressive economic positions may not align on market approaches
alignment.engagement_strategy: Lean into transparency and accountability framing — Schiff's core brand. Contact on federal housing votes and government reform bills. Push for federal housing data transparency requirements.

officials.name: Lou Correa
officials.title: U.S. Representative, CD-46

contact.office: 2323 N Broadway, Suite 210, Santa Ana, CA 92706

alignment.label: Mixed Ally
alignment.summary: Moderate Democrat representing a diverse OC district. More centrist than progressive — closer to Doug's pragmatic approach. Focus on constituent services over ideology.
key_agreements: Moderate Democrat aligned on pragmatic governance
key_disagreements: May not be a strong housing supply advocate specifically
Immigration focus may deprioritize housing/transit issues
alignment.engagement_strategy: YOUR direct House representative — highest-impact federal relationship. Attend town halls. Push for pro-supply housing positions at the federal level. Correa is a moderate who responds to constituent input — specific, data-backed asks about housing and transit funding work best. Note: Prop 50 redistricting may change district boundaries.

officials.notes: New Prop 50 redistricting maps may change district boundaries significantly.

officials.name: Tom Umberg
officials.title: State Senator, SD-34

contact.office: 1600 E 4th St, Suite 244, Santa Ana, CA 92701

alignment.label: Mixed Ally
alignment.summary: Termed out — Avelino Valencia (current Assemblymember) is frontrunner for this seat. Umberg has been moderate and pragmatic, generally aligned on housing enforcement.
key_disagreements: Limited housing-specific legislative record
alignment.engagement_strategy: Lame duck — termed out. Focus on evaluating Valencia (frontrunner successor) on housing and transit positions. Check Valencia's Assembly voting record on SB 9, RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy. June 2026 primary is the key date.

officials.notes: TERMED OUT. Open seat 2026. Avelino Valencia (D, current AD-68 Assemblymember) is frontrunner. June 2026 primary.

officials.name: Avelino Valencia (running for Senate)
officials.title: State Assemblymember, AD-68

alignment.label: Mixed
alignment.summary: Running for SD-34 Senate seat. 4 years in Assembly focusing on consumer protection, gun violence reduction, government transparency. Housing record needs evaluation.
key_agreements: Generally pro-development as a Democrat in a purple district
key_disagreements: Housing-specific positions unclear — needs research
May not be a strong YIMBY champion
alignment.engagement_strategy: CRITICAL to evaluate before June 2026 primary. Research his Assembly voting record on housing bills. Attend any candidate forums. If aligned, early support in a competitive primary has high leverage. AD-68 will also be an open seat — watch for candidates from Santa Ana city council.

officials.notes: Running for SD-34 Senate seat. AD-68 will be open. Multiple Santa Ana city council candidates expected.

officials.name: Vicente Sarmiento (D) or Donald Wagner (R)
officials.title: OC Board Supervisor, District 2 or 3

contact.office: Hall of Administration, 333 W Santa Ana Blvd, Santa Ana, CA 92701

alignment.label: TBD
alignment.summary: Exact district depends on specific address (near boundary of Districts 2 and 3). Sarmiento (D, Dist 2) is more aligned; Wagner (R, Dist 3) is less aligned. Critical to determine which district once address is confirmed.
key_agreements: If Sarmiento: generally pro-housing, part of Democratic majority coalition
If Wagner: some pro-business development positions
key_disagreements: If Wagner: Republican — may oppose housing mandates and transit investment
alignment.engagement_strategy: FIRST PRIORITY: Determine your exact supervisor district at ocvote.gov/maps once you have an address. This determines whether you have an ally (Sarmiento) or an opponent (Wagner) on the board. Either way, attend Tuesday BoS meetings and submit public comments on housing items.

officials.notes: Need specific address to determine district. Near boundary of Districts 2 (Sarmiento, D) and 3 (Wagner, R).

officials.name: TBD
officials.title: City of Orange Council Member

contact.office: City Hall, 300 E Chapman Ave, Orange, CA 92866

alignment.label: Unknown
alignment.summary: Need specific address to determine district. 6 district-elected members + directly elected mayor. 3 council seats + mayor on Nov 2026 ballot. Housing Element compliance is the defining issue.
alignment.engagement_strategy: Once district is determined, research your council member's voting record on housing and development items. The Housing Element and Builder's Remedy applications are the litmus test issues. Nov 2026 election is an opportunity to shape the council on housing.

officials.notes: Need specific address for district. 3 seats + mayor on Nov 2026 ballot. Filing period TBD.

officials.name: Orange Unified School District Board
officials.title: School Board

alignment.label: Mixed
alignment.summary: Successful recall of culture-war board members in 2024 has stabilized the district. New board is more pragmatic. School quality intersects with housing — good schools support density.
key_agreements: Shared interest in community stability and growth
key_disagreements: School districts sometimes resist density near campuses
alignment.engagement_strategy: Lower priority but monitor. The 2024 recall was a significant win for pragmatic governance. Support continued good-governance candidates. Use OUSD data on enrollment and school quality in housing advocacy — healthy schools need housing-stable families.

officials.notes: Successful recall of culture-war board members in 2024. District is stabilizing.

officials.name: OCTA Board
officials.title: Orange County Transportation Authority

alignment.label: Ally
alignment.summary: Overseeing OC Streetcar launch (Spring 2026) and Metrolink commuter rail. Board appointed by elected officials — composition reflects political balance. Transit investment is a shared priority.
key_agreements: OC Streetcar implementation (95% complete, active testing)
Transit-oriented development potential along streetcar corridor
key_disagreements: OC is auto-centric — OCTA still prioritizes road projects
Limited political appetite for major transit expansion beyond streetcar
alignment.engagement_strategy: Monitor board meetings and push for TOD (transit-oriented development) along the new streetcar corridor. The OC Streetcar launch is a once-in-a-generation opportunity to demonstrate transit value in a car-centric county. Support ridership and advocate for corridor upzoning.

officials.notes: OC Streetcar launching Spring 2026 (4.15 miles, Santa Ana to Garden Grove). 95% complete.
